skill.name: compound-docs
skill.description: Capture solved technical problems as searchable solution documentation, including categories, symptoms, root causes, related fixes, and reusable patterns.
skill.summary: Turn confirmed fixes into searchable team knowledge and recurring solution patterns.
skill.author: Bind

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found no malicious code, prompt injection, credential access, or data exfiltration. Most alerts are false positives from Markdown backticks, sample shell commands, schema text, and a prior-art GitHub link. The skill does intentionally edit local documentation files and may run local search or directory commands, so it carries medium operational risk and should be used in trusted workspaces.

medium_findings.title: Intentional Local Documentation Writes
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to add enum values to schema.yaml, create category directories, create solution documents, and append recurring issues to patterns.md. This is expected behavior for a documentation skill, but it can modify repository files and should be used only where local file changes are acceptable.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited instructions explicitly direct local file and directory creation. The behavior is legitimate for the skill purpose, but the filesystem impact is clear.

medium_findings.title: Auto Trigger Can Start a File-Changing Workflow
medium_findings.description: The skill auto-triggers on common confirmation phrases and then checks whether a solved problem should be documented. This is not malicious, but casual confirmations could initiate a documentation workflow that later writes local files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The trigger phrases and workflow are explicit in the instructions. The risk depends on host tool behavior and user consent before final file writes.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Misclassified as Code Execution
low_findings.description: The Ruby backtick and shell execution alerts occur in Markdown prose, inline code, fenced examples, and templates. They are not bundled executable code, although some examples such as grep, ls, and mkdir may be run by an agent during normal use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relevant files are documentation files, and the suspicious tokens appear in prose or Markdown code fences. No standalone script or hidden execution path was found.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Is a Prior-Art Reference
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL points to the public GitHub project that inspired the skill. It is a documentation link, not a network request or exfiltration endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both occurrences are Markdown links in descriptive text. No evidence found that the skill contacts the URL or sends data there.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Text Pattern False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings map to schema descriptions, Markdown frontmatter, and template text. No hashing, encryption, random generation, or credential handling logic is present in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain natural-language descriptions or schema metadata. No evidence found of any cryptographic implementation.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Alerts Are Local Documentation Context
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance alerts map to schema examples and instructions to read or search local documentation. They do not gather host secrets, enumerate sensitive system state, or contact external services.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are instructions or examples tied to documentation validation and prevention notes. No evidence found of broad host reconnaissance or sensitive data collection.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Shell Commands in Agent Instructions
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes shell examples for grep, ls, and mkdir. These are ordinary documentation operations, but agents should avoid interpolating untrusted text into commands without quoting or review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible and local, with no malicious endpoint or secret access. Risk remains because future user-supplied values could become command arguments if implemented carelessly.

content.user_title: Document Solved Issues With Reusable Patterns
content.value_statement: Teams often solve the same technical problem more than once because fixes stay buried in chat history. This skill turns confirmed fixes into searchable solution docs and promotes repeated issues into patterns.
actual_capabilities: Detects confirmation phrases such as "that worked" and starts a documentation workflow.
Collects symptoms, category, component, root cause, solution, and prevention details.
Uses schema.yaml to validate frontmatter and extend enum values when needed.
Searches existing solution docs for related problems before creating a new entry.
Creates category-based Markdown solution files and can update patterns.md for recurring issues.
limitations: It depends on conversation context and may ask for missing details.
It does not prove that a fix is correct without user confirmation.
It writes local documentation files, so review changes before committing.
It is designed for engineering knowledge capture, not full incident management.

use_cases.title: Capture Debugging Wins
use_cases.description: Document the symptom, failed attempts, working fix, and prevention steps after a bug is resolved.

use_cases.title: Build Team Knowledge Bases
use_cases.description: Create consistent solution records that new team members can search during future development work.

use_cases.title: Promote Recurring Patterns
use_cases.description: Identify repeated deployment, testing, integration, or performance issues and surface approved solutions.

prompt_templates.title: Document This Fix
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner prompt after a confirmed fix
prompt_templates.prompt: That worked. Use compound-docs to document the problem, the fix, and how to prevent it next time.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Complete Solution Note
prompt_templates.prompt: Use compound-docs for this resolved issue. Include symptoms, category, component, root cause, solution, what did not work, and prevention.

prompt_templates.title: Find Related Issues First
prompt_templates.prompt: Before creating a new solution doc, search existing solution docs for related symptoms, files, dependencies, and error messages.

prompt_templates.title: Promote a Repeated Pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: Review existing solution docs for this recurring issue. If it meets the promotion threshold, update patterns.md with the wrong approach, correct approach, and when it applies.

output_examples.input: A Docker build failed in CI and was fixed by pinning the base image and clearing stale cache.
output_examples.output: A deployment solution note with symptoms, root cause, failed attempts, the working fix, prevention guidance, and Docker-related tags.

output_examples.input: An API integration failed because an auth header was missing, and a similar issue may already exist.
output: A search summary of related solution docs.
A new integration note only if the issue is distinct.
A related-doc reference when an existing fix is connected.

output_examples.input: The same test flake has appeared three times across solution docs.
output_examples.output: A pattern entry that explains the repeated failure mode, the reliable fix, and the conditions where the pattern applies.

best_practices: Confirm the fix actually worked before creating a solution document.
Record specific symptoms and error messages so future searches find the doc.
Review generated file changes before committing them to the repository.
anti_patterns: Do not document simple typos or obvious one-line mistakes as reusable knowledge.
Do not add vague symptoms such as "it broke" without observable details.
Do not promote a pattern until related issues show repeated evidence.

faq.question: When does this skill activate?
faq.answer: It activates when a user confirms a fix worked or manually invokes the compound command.

faq.question: What files does it create?
faq.answer: It creates Markdown solution documents under the configured output directory and may update patterns.md.

faq.question: Does it require a specific technology stack?
faq.answer: No. The categories are general, and the schema can grow with the project.

faq.question: Can it update the schema?
faq.answer: Yes. It may add new category, component, or root cause values when existing enums do not fit.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium-risk warning because it intentionally writes local documentation files and includes local shell examples.

faq.question: What should users review?
faq.answer: Users should review newly created docs, schema changes, and pattern updates before committing them.
